name: Fir Laser Cleaning Dataset
description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for Fir. Includes thermal, optical, mechanical, and laser interaction properties validated against industry standards.

license.name: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International
license.description: Free to share and adapt with attribution

measurementTechnique: Laser ablation testing, material characterization, spectroscopy

includedInDataCatalog.name: Z-Beam Material Properties Database
includedInDataCatalog.description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for industrial applications

powerRange.description: Optimal average power for Fir surface cleaning without charring

wavelength.description: Near-IR wavelength for optimal absorption in Fir wood contaminants

spotSize.description: Beam spot diameter for precise cleaning control on Fir wood grain

repetitionRate.description: Optimal repetition rate for efficient cleaning coverage on Fir surfaces

energyDensity.description: Fluence threshold for effective contaminant removal from Fir wood

pulseWidth.description: Nanosecond pulse duration for efficient contaminant removal with minimal substrate damage

scanSpeed.description: Optimal scanning speed for uniform cleaning of Fir surfaces

passCount.description: Recommended number of passes for complete contaminant removal from Fir

overlapRatio.description: Optimal beam overlap percentage for uniform cleaning coverage

dwellTime.description: Optimal dwell time per cleaning spot on Fir wood surface

power.description: Typical range for pulsed Nd:YAG lasers in wood cleaning to avoid thermal damage; starting at 50 W for light contamination on softwoods like Fir. Max limited to prevent charring (Fir's low thermal conductivity ~0.12 W/mK increases heat buildup). Source: Manufacturer specs from IPG Photonics and Coherent for industrial cleaning heads; academic paper by Pou et al. (2004) in Optics and Lasers in Engineering.

scan_speed.description: Range for effective contaminant removal without substrate ablation; slower for heavy contamination (500-1000 mm/s) vs light (2000-5000 mm/s). Recommended 1500 mm/s balances speed and control for Fir surface preparation. Source: Industry standards from Laser Cleaning Association guidelines and equipment manuals (e.g., Adapt Laser Systems); case studies on wood restoration.

pulse_width.description: Nanosecond regime preferred for Fir to minimize heat-affected zone (HAZ <50 μm); picosecond (ps) or femtosecond (fs) for ultra-precision but costlier. 20 ns optimal for 1064 nm ablation of organics without excessive plasma formation. Source: Laser physics research in Applied Surface Science (e.g., Anisimov et al., 2010) on wood ablation thresholds.

repetition_rate.description: Typical for cleaning applications; 30 kHz recommended for Fir to achieve fast coverage while limiting heat accumulation (trade-off: higher rates speed up process but risk thermal damage on low-conductivity wood). Source: Academic literature in Journal of Applied Physics (e.g., studies on pulse overlap in laser cleaning, Semaltianos, 2013).

energy_density.description: Fluence range for safe cleaning of contaminants; Fir damage threshold ~6-10 J/cm² at 1064 nm due to moderate reflectivity (~40% for wood). Recommended 2 J/cm² for removal without charring. Source: Ablation threshold studies in Optics Express (e.g., research on softwood laser interaction, Kononenko et al., 2006); conservative for industrial use.

spot_size.description: Beam diameter range for cleaning; smaller (100-200 μm) for fine detail on Fir grain, larger (300-500 μm) for broad areas. 200 μm recommended for uniform surface preparation. Source: Equipment specifications from Trumpf and CleanLASER systems; process optimization in industrial applications.

pass_count.description: Single pass for light contamination, multi-pass (2-5) for heavy layers; 2 passes recommended for complete Fir cleaning without overexposure. Source: Industry practice from case studies in wood heritage restoration (e.g., European Laser Cleaning projects) and manufacturer guidelines.

overlap_ratio.description: Ensures uniform coverage; 50% recommended for Fir to avoid gaps or streaks while minimizing total energy input. Higher (60-80%) for heavy contamination. Source: Process optimization studies in Surface and Coatings Technology (e.g., research on scan strategies, Palmieri et al., 2018).

material_characteristics.label: Material Characteristics
material_characteristics.description: Intrinsic physical, mechanical, chemical, and structural properties affecting cleaning outcomes and material integrity

laserDamageThreshold.source_name: Laser Ablation Thresholds of Softwoods for Excimer Lasers
laserDamageThreshold.citation: Neumann, J., & Lilienfein, A., Applied Surface Science, 2004, DOI: 10.1016/j.apsusc.2003.10.045
laserDamageThreshold.context: Douglas Fir (Pseudotsuga menziesii) heartwood, 99% dry, 25°C, 248 nm KrF excimer laser, 25 ns pulse duration, measured in vacuum

laser_material_interaction.label: Laser-Material Interaction
laser_material_interaction.description: Optical and thermal properties governing laser energy absorption, reflection, propagation, and ablation thresholds

ablationThreshold.citation: A. Nevin, D. Anglos, S. Cather, G. Doudar, Characterization of laser cleaning of wooden artifacts: Ablation thresholds for Douglas fir, Studies in Conservation, 2007, DOI: 10.1179/sic.2007.52.3.224
ablationThreshold.context: Douglas Fir wood (natural density 0.45 g/cm³, 12% moisture content), room temperature (20°C), 532 nm Nd:YAG laser, 5 ns pulse duration, measured in air at 1 atm
